title: Compare area with unit squares
description: Compare the amount of unit squares that cover figures.

assessment_items.question_content:
Each square in the grid is a unit square with an area of $1$ square unit.  

**Fill in the blanks.**

Figure $\blueD{\text{A}}$ covers [[☃ dropdown 1]] unit squares on the grid than figure $\maroonD{\text{B}}$.

So, the area of figure $\blueD{\text{A}}$ is  [[☃ dropdown 2]]  than the area of figure $\maroonD{\text{B}}$.


[[☃ image 1]]
hints:
The area is the amount of flat space a shape takes up. 

We can find the area by counting the number of unit squares that the shape covers.

$\blueD{\text{Figure A}}$ has an area of $\blueD{7}$ square units.


[[☃ image 1]]

$\maroonD{\text{Figure B}}$ has an area of $\maroonD{8}$ square units.


[[☃ image 1]]
$\blueD{7}\text{ square units} < \maroonD{8}\text{ square units}$

Figure $\blueD{\text{A}}$ covers *fewer* unit squares on the grid than figure $\maroonD{\text{B}}$.

So, the area of figure $\blueD{\text{A}}$ is *less* than the area of figure $\maroonD{\text{B}}$.

options.alt: A grid with 2 shapes of different sizes labeled A and B. Shape A with 4 rows and 2 columns of 7 unit squares connected together on a grid. First, second, and fourth rows from the top have 2 unit squares each and the third row from the top has 1 unit square. Shape B with 2 rows and 5 columns of 8 unit squares connected together on a grid. The top row has 3 unit squares and the bottom row has 5 unit squares.

assessment_items.question_content:
Each square in the grid is a unit square with an area of $1$ square unit.  

**Fill in the blanks.**

hints:
The area is the amount of flat space a shape takes up. 

We can find the area by counting the number of unit squares that the shape covers.

$\blueD{\text{Rectangle A}}$ has an area of $\blueD6$ square units.


[[☃ image 1]]

$\maroonD{\text{Rectangle B}}$ has an area of $\maroonD{4}$ square units.


[[☃ image 1]]
$\blueD{6}\text{ square units} > \maroonD{4}\text{ square units}$

Rectangle $\blueD{\text{A}}$ covers *more* unit squares on the grid than rectangle $\maroonD{\text{B}}$.

So, the area of rectangle $\blueD{\text{A}}$ is *greater* than the area of rectangle $\maroonD{\text{B}}$.

options.alt: A grid with 2 rectangles of different sizes labeled A and B. Rectangle A covers 6 rows and 1 column of the grid. Rectangle B covers 2 rows and 2 columns of the grid.

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which rectangle has the greatest area?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

**How do we know which rectangle has the greatest area?**

[[☃ radio 2]]
hints: The area is the amount of flat space a shape takes up. We can find the area by counting the number of unit squares that the shape covers.

This rectangle has an area of $\blueD{12}$ square units. 


[[☃ image 1]]

This rectangle has an area of $\goldD{10}$ square units.

[[☃ image 1]]
$\blueD{12}\text{ square units} > \goldD{10}\text{ square units}$

### Answers:

This rectangle has the greatest area:  


[[☃ image 1]]

We know which rectangle has the greatest area because *it covers the most space*.

choices.content: ![A rectangle has 4 rows. Each row has 3 unit squares.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/bde4a7df0ea93d102106c0c88f38dfeed93b2fcb)
![A rectangle has 5 rows. Each row has 2 unit squares.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/0378df9222a4a9fe54b0b7da782c8f01f65fa711)
It has the most columns of square units.

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which rectangle has the larger area?**



[[☃ image 1]]



[[☃ image 2]]

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: The area is the amount of flat space a shape takes up. We can find the area by counting the number of unit squares that the shape covers.

Rectangle A has an area of $\greenD{12}$ square units.


[[☃ image 1]]

Rectangle B has an area of  $\blueD{12}$ square units.


[[☃ image 1]]
$\greenD{12}\text{ square units} = \blueD{12}\text{ square units}$
Both rectangles have the same area.

options.alt: Rectangle A has 4 rows. Each row has 3 unit squares.
Rectangle B has 2 rows. Each row has 6 unit squares.

choices.content: Both rectangles have the same area.

$\maroonD{11}\text{ square units} < \blueD{14}\text{ square units}$
Figure B has the larger area.

options.alt: Figure A has 3 rows and 6 columns of 11 unit squares connected together. Top row has 6 unit squares, middle row has 3 unit squares, and the bottom row has 2 unit squares.
Figure B has 5 rows and 4 columns of 14 unit squares connected together. The top 2 rows have 1 unit square each and the bottom 3 rows have 4 unit squares each.

choices.content: Both figures have the same area.
